speaker: interviewer
text: Camp, I thought they were building barracks.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was barracks, but they're wooden barracks. It was not-- it was, you know, it's like a temporary thing. They said they are bringing in the prisoners of war and we-- we-- we-- we wanted to believe, because sometimes your mind does not want to-- to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you want to just ID the tape with her name and then run the tape.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure, I will, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: Three seconds, two seconds left. Yeah, go ahead. Let it run until he's done. There's no rush. It doesn't have to be 30 seconds. If it's-- if it's in a minute and a half, it doesn't make any difference. Just ID the tape if you would. Just say her name and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Brenda Senders, tape two.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's it. Good, thank you. Speed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's continue with the story, Brenda.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know realize that something is wrong with the people that they're taking out of your ghetto. What is-- what was it that you decided to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: When came our turn, I was almost to the end of the-- of the group, because my name was Szyr, all my family name. So my family and everybody were almost to the end. This German was standing down there and-- with a big list of the people who are living in the ghetto. And he was marking off anybody who was not present, marking it off. It did not make sense at that time to us. And then, like it would be somebody would go on an outing, on a-- on a-- on a-- some-- just for a-- for a-- some kind of-- you know, he was marking it off. Then we find out why did they did it. Because they wanted to know how many people remained and how many they have to still look for to be killed. And then I remember right behind the gate, there was this-- we were surrounded with the-- with the Nazi was-- was going-- and-- and this guy who was standing down there and marking off the name, his face was red, I remember, and I think he stunk with alcohol. He couldn't, I bet he couldn't stomach maybe this whole thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: When they took us in behind the gate, we were surrounded by the Ukrainian and-- and this German, and I remember he was wearing a black armband with a white skeleton. And one woman-- brief-- came up to him and she said, where are you taking us? He said, we are taking you to a working camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this where they took us in the outskirts of our town, where this camp was erected. And there where I first saw that camp. There were three big barracks, wooden barracks, a toilet for men open on one side, a toilet for woman, double barbed wire. People already were-- when we-- when they brought us in, the people from the small town they brought them in a night before, a day before, and they were already down there. And we saw already dead bodies by the barbed wire, double barbed wire, by the barbed wire, and the blood was already dry. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So they pushed us in, in that enclosure. The-- the Germans with the machine guns was sitting on the hill, facing us between this-- this double barbed wire with a German, a Ukrainian with guns. They got all the Jews from-- from Sarny and from the surrounding-- OK, what happened--

speaker: interviewee
text: --towns.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened next to your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then, I start running around, and I see my relatives. I see my relatives from my small town. So I went up to a group of people, my mother with me, and I-- their name was [? Pomerance. ?] They have relatives and their grandchildren right in Washington. I came up to them and my great uncle, this saint of a man, was sitting down there on the ground and praying to God. And I guess God was not with him and against him. And the whole family surrounded him. And my mother with me standing down there-- and they called out because they're-- they're-- they were very well prepared and very, very organized, the Nazis, but this is was concerned.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right up there they called out the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], because alphabetically, B must come to the-- to the gate. They all stood up. My mother, there was a daughter, a son I remember, the whole family. I ask him-- my mother and me ask him-- Leah was the daughter's name. Leah, what is this? She said this is my wedding gown. This is my fiance. I said, where are you going? She said, I don't know. They picked themselves up and they went, and that's the last I seen of them.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have lost my father down there. I don't know. My father had a terrible temper. He spoke perfect German. Maybe he went right away to the gate-- gate and he start maybe a fight or asking some questions. He was killed, right maybe there on the spot, because I haven't seen him. Then another family went up from a smaller, another town. I used to go on vacation down there, come to there. [? Dabrowica. ?] Right away, they got up. There was the mother, the daughter, and oh, did she stand out. She was a beautiful girl. Sarah was her name. My age, maybe a year younger. Beautiful blue eyes, dark hair. She looked like Elizabeth Taylor. I always compare her.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, where is your father? 'Cause I knew he used to come. She said, my father escaped. She said, my father escaped at night. We don't know if he's alive or dead. I met him in the woods afterwards, and he died fighting the Germans. That's not a consolation. Sarah, got up with her mother with the townspeople. And I said, Sarah, where are you going? She said, I don't know and I don't care. I want it to be all over it. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Then penetrated right there and then through the camp that we are going to cut the barbed wire. That's it. That's the end. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And there was this guy Migdal, and I'll call him a hero. There were two guys. One is shorter, I don't remember. I need a family name. He was Migdal, this guy, this tall, and handsome, and blond guy. And he looked more Aryan than-- than-- than Hitler could only imagine. His only crime was he was a Polish national of Jewish faith. And he was trapped down there. He has already escaped once from the claws of the Germans. I went up to my mother. And there was in the sea of this hate-- it's-- it's-- it's-- you have to understand-- there was this German, my father's age, maybe a little bit older, was standing with this gun. And this German was not SS. He was put on there with a gun and they ask him to watch those criminals. And it's down on him. He had some humanity in him. And my friend-- I saw her for the first time in my life-- went up to him. To talk to him. He had the right to kill her right then. I'm behind her. And he looked at her and I'm behind her. And he said, oh, my god. They-- they-- [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] they-- they kill you all. [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] from here. Run from her-- from-- from here, you dummy Jews. But we knew at that time. That's it. That's the end. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And there are other things. There is the doctor. I was sitting down there. My dentist, oh, she once escaped from the Germans, wind up in Sarny and remained. And she was thrown in. But the Germans, in the-- after we were brought in and under, her little boy of six or seven was taken from her hand and on a telephone pole was killed. And she was throwing in her husband and the boy. She was throwing in-- in the camp. And I remember she was sitting down there, and she had some needles to-- to die. She had seven, eight, or nine. I don't know. She had a certain amount. She wanted-- she left one for herself. And she said, anybody who wants to stand in line-- or standing, people were standing in line to actually to die instead of being killed by the Germans. I went up to my mother, and actually, next to this German, the barbed wire was cut. This good, this-- this nice. The Ukrainian [INAUDIBLE]-- and they were-- they were nasty-- had to be bribed. And I remember we put in some-- in some gold. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Anybody who had some trinkets put it in. I'm sorry. I'm a tough lady, don't worry. And-- and they put in a handkerchief, and they had to bring to him and they told him. I remember my-- my friend's father took off her boot, and he said, bring us some water so you'll get a drink for the gold. But actually, we wanted to play for time. We wanted water, because it was a hot day, but anyway, we wanted to play for time.

speaker: interviewer
text: I went up to my mother, and I said, Momma, I'm going to run. She looked at here. She looked at me, and she said-- if you-- she said, where is the decency of the world? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And she said, if you remain alive, you should never forget. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's what I am here.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then and there, that's maybe my pledge or maybe my promise, or maybe my hate. I said if I'll survive I'll fight. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] OK, I'm sorry. And if I'll have to die, I'll die with a gun in my hands. And I did it all.

speaker: interviewer
text: You went through the barbed wire then?

speaker: interviewee
text: The barbed wire was cut.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, but you went through the wire. Who was with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went through the wire. A whole bunch of people, hordes of people, one on top of the other. Some were wounded already, some were not. Our only luck was because their own people were standing in the double barbed wire, and they could not-- they could not-- they could not kill us all at once. And we start running, so the confusion became very great. We mixed it in with the Germans and with the-- with the Ukrainians. So that was our luck. My mother seeing me behind the barbed wire, threw over my sister, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and she followed me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anybody else from your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Survived?

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, at that moment, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Escaped at that moment.

speaker: interviewee
text: That moment, no. My sister was running behind me. And I remember I was so excited in my head that she was all bloody from some other people, must be from wounded people. And she wore just a little shirt I remember. I threw the shirt and I throw it out. I took the shirt off. They recognize her. In my heat of my thinking, in the confusion of thinking, they recognize her, she's Jewish, whatever the mark was. And I throw it in a-- in a-- in a-- in one of the yards to the Polish people. And it was misery. It was inferno. It was Dante's Inferno.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you run to?

speaker: interviewee
text: My first instinct was to run to-- we had friends who were of Christian persuasion. They were good friends of ours, I told you. And my mother did a lot of things. If not for my mother and a group of people, they would have been in Siberia, those people. And they considered us like family, like-- like a daughter, like my mother would be to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the first thing-- we had some stuff with them we were hiding during the war. When the Germans came in, we went in the ghetto in case we needed something to exchange for bread or for food. So the first-- It was not far from that place where they were killing off all the people to their house. I used to sneak out from the ghetto sometimes during the day or at night, taking off my-- my junk, my yellow patch, taking off and just going through the gate. And-- and going actually on the-- pretend being of non-Jewish persuasion and bring some food back to the-- to the ghetto. So the first thing I think is to run down there. When I run down there with my sister, Sima was there, I run into the house. I ask her for water. She gave us some water. I said, hide us. She could not. She said, I'm afraid. I could understand. It was on the-- you understand if she would hide us, she would be killed together with us. It was her life and our life.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then they were sitting in there. There was a little-- a little, like, a yard fenced in. And I opened for some reason, and there, her husband. With some more Poles are standing like nothing would happen. They are telling jokes. And this you-- this is-- this is-- stands in my head forever. Telling jokes and laughing on a high voice. When they saw us, we must have looked like-- like-- like beast, like-- like-- you know, like-- like animals from-- from escapees. I was barefooted, all from the-- from the barbed wire scratched up. My sister, half-naked. And they looked at us, and they stopped laughing right there and then. It hit them straight then. They [INAUDIBLE] They looked at us. We looked at them. We didn't say, exchange, not a word. They saw it.

speaker: interviewer
text: But this I want you to say, you see. They knew it. Their friends and nobody raised a voice. That's the-- that's the horrible and the atrocious things that happened. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] From there? Where'd you go? Where'd you go next?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wanted to go into the village where my grandparents-- they lived in another village. My grandparents lived in Karpalivka, we call it. And I wanted them there. I knew it's away from the town, and there is a chance I can-- I can hide. Ironic from then there, I split with my sister. My sister, somehow she strolled away from me and I-- and I lost her. Not-- I could not wait for any moments. I said, she'll come later. I could not wait for any moments to-- to lose. I went away. And I came to a guy, to a village man and a friend of my grandparents, because I knew he was a very [? gullible ?] guy, and I-- and I guessed it right. When I walked in down there, my sister was down there. It's like fiction. And my uncle was down there. My uncle escaped also the same time as I did. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] From there, he give us food. He took us to a barn. We were down there four days, five days. I don't remember. And then my uncle decided you are women. He gave us peasant clothes. I spoke the language well and I could-- I could mix in between the peasants if they didn't know. Somehow they would smell out, they would know. I would tell them I'm from another village, but they would know. They-- they would-- they would know my parents or something. But anyway, I-- I-- they gave us some peasant clothes. And I remember the first time I started learning how to the-- the-- the-- the [INAUDIBLE] they call it. This is like during the Roman times, you know the-- the thing. But you make it from leek in our-- in our part of the town, in our part of the country, from leek. And then you make it and it looks like sandals. And then with drops, you rub it in and then, that's the way you wear it. And that's the way I used-- I used to look like a peasant, no-- no-- no different. From there we went-- we went into-- into a-- a house, who was standing-- it's a hutor, they call it. It's a house by itself. It's like a farm. We call it here a farm. It's a house in the field, surrounded by their fields. And then they use to work on the fields, and they lived on there.

speaker: interviewer
text: I went to their house, and at night, I knocked on the door, and the-- and the dogs are barking the hell out. And I knocked on the door. I told them who I was. They are scared of people. You know, it's like-- it's like scared of us, you know? And I told them who I was. And he said, OK, we'll put you into bins. I remember she took us out in the field and she hide us for a day or two.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then and then we decided, with my sister, that we'll go to Karasin. Karasin was away from the town, deep in the woods. Right already, it-- it's more White Russia. It's into-- deep into the woods. And down there we have a more chance. We heard in the ghetto there are-- there are Partisans. There are rumors. All kind of rumors were going around. And there, where we decide-- I said to my sister, we are going to that. We are going down there. And one night, we went at night. And everything at night had to be done. And I remember at night, we were going by a little stream, by a little river. And at night, I heard on the other side of the-- of the stream people are washing their hands, and I got so-- I became so excited, so happy. I thought this must be people like ours. And I wanted to speak out. But then, you know, intuitively sometimes-- I said to my sister, quiet. Let's hear what's going on. They were thieves going out at night, and going out to my village, where my family lived, coming out with some honey, some things-- some honey and some-- some other goodies. Who knows. And one to the other one said in Ukrainian, let's get up. Let's go from here, because it's daybreak is coming. And then I find out that they were actually thieves. But the ironic things, you know, the-- the thieves and the things sometimes used to help those unfortunate people, us. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened next? What happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: From there, it took us a whole day. We went through the woods. The woods-- during the day we used to hide, and at night we used to walk on the-- on the--

speaker: interviewer
text: On the road.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the road. Yes, exactly. Until we came to Karasin. We came to Karasin. We find out that Shimon is down there-- there's a lot of Jews escaped. And I start begging, take us into the group of Shimon. They didn't want us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was Shimon?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shimon was a friend of our family. And he also escaped from the graves. He escaped when they were killing off all his family. He escaped from there, and he ran into Karasin. That was his hometown where he lived down there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was Shimon's last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Paikow.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: Paikow, this I remember. He was a saint of a man. So I insisted, and I said, I must see Shimon Paikow. He was a family-- he was a friend of our family. I said, if Shimon will tell me to go, I'll go. But I, you know, I was insisting. And Shimon used to go around between the village people, bring us some news. Bring us what's going on, on the front and whatever that's going on. He acquired already a gun. He and-- and Orshanski-- was Orshanski with him. Orshanski escaped, too. He was from-- from-- also from that village, from Karasin But they took him away to a ghetto to [POLISH] or someplace. Anyway, he wind up also escaping the same day like we did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Shimon was a leader of a Partisan?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of the-- of the group, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How large?

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was this small group of Jews who were down there, and he was like supervising. He was the leader of us. And then, I said, no. I'll wait for Shimon Paikow. And Shimon Paikow came up at night. I came up to him, and I said, I'm the daughter of so and so. Tears stood up in his eyes, and he said, they are like my kids, and they will stay with us. And then he said it was the law because he was the leader of the group . I stayed with them. And the-- the people from the village had great respect for him. And they knew that if something-- if they will do something bad, they will be paid back. And the story about this-- and all of a sudden, while we were down there, a-- a teacher appeared into this remote village in the woods, a remote village. The village consisted, I don't know, a few hundred maybe residents, maybe not even that many, a small-- all of a sudden appeared a-- a-- a teacher that supposedly, the Germans are sending him down there to organize a school. All of a sudden, they were preoccupied to give schooling and to educate people, when the killing and the murdering was going on in the thousands and in the thousands. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: We knew right away that we must watch this foreigner, this stranger. Who is he and what? We were right away suspicious who he was. We find out exactly where he was asleep, in what house he was living.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we saw him going to Sarny, to the town in the middle of-- of the-- of the week, coming back. It was unheard. You know, you don't go around at that time when the war was raging, and the wars are going. We knew right away he wants to tip off the Germans and to find out there are Jews, there are Partisans, whatever that. It was a danger, not just to us, but for the people of the small town, of the small village, of this small village. Shimon Paikow decided that he must be eliminated. We got rid of him so he could walk no more and could not do any harm to us. From that point, I wanted to join a big group. This was not-- I wanted to fight. I heard-- we heard Partisans are getting organized.

speaker: interviewer
text: You asked me who were the Partisans. Who were there? What kind of-- who are the people? They were escapees from the ghettos. They were prisoners of war. And some people who couldn't stomach the Nazis, who were against the Nazis.

speaker: interviewee
text: They organized. And the-- the-- the escapees, the-- the POWs escaped from hell and they were murdering just like the Jews. They were murdering them just like they were murdering the Jews. In his camp, he tells you exactly what he's going to do, but people didn't pay attention. He did it, all right. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And there-- I heard it's a big group are coming through the woods. In a [NON-ENGLISH], we call it. A [NON-ENGLISH]-- a [NON-ENGLISH]. It's immovable They were [NON-ENGLISH]. They were [NON-ENGLISH]-- I have-- in Russian, I am telling you. [NON-ENGLISH], it means local. They were sitting down there in the woods in one place and then go out on assignments, just rob the trains and do hell, whatever they could. And we were, this group, what I'm talking, what I joined it was a moveable. When I walked up with my friend down there, I saw the people. I saw, you know, the people on-- the Partisans on horses with guns, machine guns, with grenades. Even we had anti-tank. The [NON-ENGLISH], we called it, [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: I liked it. And right away, I remember, strolling to me was one-- a command-- an officer. He had-- he had a officer's uniform, as I remember. He strolled up to me, and I said, officer, I wanted to go to fight. He said, would you fight? I said, oh, yes. He said, I'm not in authority to tell you yes or no. He went to his-- to his general, because he was with the general. And he said, if you wanted to fight, now or never. Do you remember the name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now!

speaker: interviewer
text: Do remember the name of the general?

speaker: interviewee
text: Naumov, General Naumov.

speaker: interviewer
text: Naumov?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was Major-- General Major. It's correct in English? General Major.

speaker: interviewer
text: Major General, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Major General.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: [RUSSIAN] it's in Russian. You say [NON-ENGLISH] but in-- in English it's--

speaker: interviewer
text: It's Major General.

speaker: interviewee
text: Major General, yes. He-- he was a major starting with us, and then he got his ranks while he was organizing the Partisans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, your sister, at that point, was eight or nine years old. What-- what did you plan to do with her?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister became a shepherd down there in the woods by this family where we were living. And when I came to this-- she was down there, living in this-- with this family, a lot of Partisans. And the people, they like it or didn't like it, they had to help us because already we had guns. And they were thinking they had to be on our side. But they were anyway on our side, because they saw already the right-- the true face of the Germans, of the Nazis. And we went and I left word for my sister that I'm going to fight. But I was not sure she knows, she doesn't know. So we went-- we stopped in a village, and I was assigned to this-- to this group. We were six of us, smaller group and then a bigger group of-- and then a bigger group of 250. [INAUDIBLE], they have names for it. I don't know in the English version how is it.

speaker: interviewer
text: When I came to this village, I came to my immediate commanding officer and with tears in my eyes, I said, I left my sister, and she will not know. She'll think that I am-- I am killed. How can I let-- how can I let her know that I am fighting, and I am with the Partisans?

speaker: interviewee
text: He said I have-- I have a solution. We are going to stay in here for two, three days. I'll give you my adjutant. I'll give you my horse. Go back to her. Tell her what's happening. And that's the story. I went-- I went back. It took us a long time. It was 50, 60 kilometers on a horse. It was a long way. We came to the village. I came to the woman. I-- I-- we go into the house, and I said I'm going to fight. I didn't have my gun yet, but she saw the other guy with the gun, with the horse. She knew that I was going to fight. If something will happen to my sister, I'll pay you. I'll burn your house, and I'll kill you. She knew it. I meant business. And the-- the-- I made my point straight and was clear.
